Strava Fitness Analysis

Instructions

The Task

  1. Explain how you define fitness.

  2. Tell me about which individual is fitter?

  3. Tell me about which individual has improved more in fitness?

  4. Make a clear and concise recommendation on which individual you would want to coach. There is no it depends here, you have to make a decision.

Write a bullet point summary on your findings at the beginning of the document.

You must use a quarto document (.qmd) and render it into html.

Metrics Meaning

  • Decompression: This means to use running as a personal wellness tool to decompress from stress

  • Max.Resp: Max.Resp = Maximum respiratory = Max rate of VO2

  • Min.Resp: Min.Resp = Minimum respiratory = Min rate of VO2

  • Min.Temp: The weather’s lowest temperature during the exercise

What is Fitness?

According to Cambridge University Press (n.d.), the definition of fitness is “the condition of being physically strong and healthy,” and Healthwise Staff (2022) expands on that definition by adding the ability to perform physical activity. Therefore, to understand what makes an individual fit, we need to understand what enables an individual to perform physical activity and which metrics we can use to track such performance. Since our analysis focuses on fitness in running, we will focus on running performance and the specific metrics used to track it.

The performance metrics that we will use are the following:

  • Stride Length: “The distance you cover with each step of your run” (Leicht, 2023). Additionally, Vasudevan (2025) suggests that knowing a person’s stride length can help detect a tendency to overstride, which can help prevent injuries, such as knee injuries.

  • Cadence: “The number of steps you take in one minute” (Leicht, 2023). Furthermore, Nike (2026) explains that cadence is important because it can be used to optimize an athlete’s ground contact time, explain abnormal stride length, and running inefficiency. For instance, a lower cadence often means longer contact time with the ground, which in turn means a longer stride length, and that could mean pushing the foot too far ahead of the athlete’s body; doing so is what causes knee injuries and results in wasted energy, or, in other words, loss of performance (Nike, 2026).

  • Aerobic Training Effect: According to Zhao (2019), Training Effect measures how each training session affects VO2 Max (Maximum Volume of Oxygen) improvement. Furthermore, Warner (2024) suggests that VO2 Max can indicate an individual’s fitness because a high VO2 Max correlates with better physical fitness and is also associated with “a lower risk for cardiovascular disease and increased longevity.” The reason VO2 Max correlates with better physical fitness and health is that it measures the amount of oxygen an athlete consumes during exercise (Warner, 2024). Oxygen consumption matters because, for a muscle to generate energy as ATP, it needs oxygen; therefore, the more oxygen the athlete can breathe in, the more energy the muscle can produce to perform the exercise (Warner, 2024).

    Zhao (2019) suggests the following description for the Training Effect ranges: (Turn this to a GT table later)

    • 0.0 - 0.9: No aerobic effect

    • 1.0 - 1.9: Minor aerobic effect

    • 2.0 - 2.9: Maintaining aerobic effect

    • 3.0 - 3.9: Improving aerobic effect

    • 4.0 - 4.9: Highly improving aerobic effect

    • 5.0: Overreaching aerobic effect

  • Heart Rate:

To determine which individual is the most fit, we will use the current performance on their metrics. However, to determine which individual we will choose to train, we will estimate each individual’s potential by forecasting their future metrics and comparing them to choose who we want to train.

Notes

  • The distance for everything is in Km, but Track Running is in meters.

  • Collie doesn’t have any data under Track Running, but I noticed that under Title in Trail Running there’s at least 1 observation with a Title description of Track Running - it’s on the last page.

  • Collie never ran Street Running or at least he doesn’t have any records of running one.

References

Cambridge University Press. (n.d.). Fitness. Cambridge Dictionary. Retrieved https://dictionary.cambridge.org/dictionary/english/fitness
Healthwise Staff. (2022, October 10). Fitness: Getting and staying active. Health Link BC. https://www.healthlinkbc.ca/healthwise/fitness-getting-and-staying-active
Leicht, L. (2023, May 4). 8 running metrics to help you track your fitness. Runners World. https://www.runnersworld.com/training/a43793832/running-metrics-guide/
Nike. (2026, January 9). What is cadence running? How to find your optimal step rate and improve performance. https://www.nike.com/ca/a/what-is-cadence-running
Vasudevan, J. (2025, February 25). Everything you need to know about stride length. Runners World. https://www.runnersworld.com/uk/training/beginners/a46820832/stride-length/
Warner, L. (2024, July 8). VO2 max: What is it and how can you improve it? Harvard Health Publishing. https://www.health.harvard.edu/staying-healthy/vo2-max-what-is-it-and-how-can-you-improve-it
Zhao, X. (2019, September 8). How should i improve my aerobic fitness? Medium. https://medium.com/@xinzhao13/understanding-how-my-wearables-understands-my-workout-285699b9deda
